Michael: Alright.  Oscar, got big plans tonight with-
Oscar: I'm on a call.
Michael: Kevin, big man, big man, what are you doing tonight?  Where are you off to?
Kevin: My brother is in town and we are going to see the Alaska Film Festival at the Scien-
Michael: Ok, alright.
Kevin: Oh.
Michael: Hey, Angela, rushy, rushy.  Where you rushin' off to?
Angela: I'm just leaving for the day.
Michael: Yeah, well duh.  Where ya headed?
Angela: Charity.  Bake drive.
Michael: Liar!
Angela: No!
Michael: You are a liar.
Angela: No, I'm not.
Michael: Dwight, oh ho, Dwight, Dwight, my loyal compadre.  You and I are hangin' tonight.  The two of us.  We are celebrating our freedom and our manhood.  You know what?  Why don't we watch that show that you've been wanting to watch, that stupid Battleship Galaxy.
Dwight: Battlestar Galatica.
Michael: That's, whatever stupid show you want-
Dwight: I can't-
Michael: To watch tonight
Dwight: Tonight.
Michael: We're watching it.
Dwight: Unfortunately, I've got plans. I have to go to practice.  Soccer practice.
Michael: I didn't know you played soccer, Dwight.
Dwight: Clarinet.
Michael: You, too, Dwight?
Dwight: Have fun tonight!  Whatever it is that you're doing, and I'll see you Monday. [to the camera]  He has no idea!
given_circumstances: The workday is ending. Michael is trying to determine who is secretly going to Jim's barbecue and whether anyone will spend time with him instead.
internal_thoughts: He is fishing and I am not participating. Every extra word is risk.
motivation: To get out without revealing anything about her real plans.
reflection: Deflection works best when I sound offended by the question itself.
scene_arc: Michael asks Pam, Oscar, Kevin, Angela, and Dwight what they are doing that night. Everyone brushes him off or lies. He tries to enlist Dwight for an evening together, but Dwight declines while privately gloating to the camera that Michael has no idea.

title: Jim offers Angela food and gets iced out instantly | distance
full_dialogue:
Jim: Angela, burger? Dog?  Havin' fun?
Angela: I got sap on me.
Jim: Chicken, hot dog, burger.
Angela: I'm a vegetarian.
Jim: There is soda inside.
Angela: Guh.
given_circumstances: The barbecue continues, and Jim is circulating among guests as host. Angela remains stiff and uncomfortable in social settings like this.
internal_thoughts: I am uncomfortable, sticky, and surrounded by casual people who confuse friendliness with access. I would like less of all of this.
motivation: To endure the party without inviting more interaction than necessary.
reflection: Jim treats this like a normal pleasant gathering. It is not normal and it is not pleasant.
scene_arc: Jim offers Angela burgers, hot dogs, and soda. Angela says she has sap on her and reminds him she is a vegetarian before ending the exchange with visible annoyance.

title: Pam asks Phyllis about secret romances and gets mistaken for being with Jim | projection
full_dialogue:
Angela: I think it's alright. Jesus drank wine. [Pam overhears]
Pam: Hey Phyllis, come here for a second.
Phyllis: Sure.
Pam: Have you heard anything about any secret office romances?
Phyllis: You tell me.  Well, you do mean you and Jim right?  Oh God.  I am so sorry, I thought, you guys hang out all the time and you're talking all the time.  I'm sorry!
Pam: That's ok.  It's ok.
given_circumstances: Pam is still hunting for evidence about Angela and Dwight at the party. She pulls Phyllis aside for help.
internal_thoughts: If I say something calm and ordinary, perhaps I remain ordinary too.
motivation: To normalize her own behavior without revealing anything deeper.
reflection: The less suspicious I sound, the better.
scene_arc: Angela comments that wine is alright because Jesus drank wine. Pam then asks Phyllis whether she has heard about any secret office romances. Phyllis assumes Pam means Pam and Jim, then apologizes when she realizes she misread the question.

title: Angela snaps at Oscar for shaking his leg | irritation
full_dialogue: Angela: [Oscar shakes his leg] You're going to have to stop that. [Oscar moves over] I can still feel it.
given_circumstances: The party continues in a cramped shared space. Angela is already tense, and Oscar's movement pushes her over the edge.
internal_thoughts: Everything is too close, too loose, too informal. If I can at least stop one annoying motion, maybe I can breathe.
motivation: To regain control over a small physical irritation in an already uncomfortable environment.
reflection: When I feel trapped, tiny disturbances become enormous.
scene_arc: Angela tells Oscar he has to stop shaking his leg. Oscar moves over, and Angela says she can still feel it.

title: Angela silently judges Phyllis's light choice | control
full_dialogue:
Angela: Is there anything we're missing? Phyllis, you got the lights?
Phyllis: Yes, I got those cute little ones. [Angela looks at her disapprovingly] Do you think I should have gotten the big ones?
Angela: We'll see.
given_circumstances: Angela heads the Party Planning Committee and has been managing every detail of the Christmas party. Phyllis is a committee member. Angela has very specific standards for how the party should be executed.
internal_thoughts: The small lights. Of course she got the small lights. I didn't specify because I thought it was obvious. This is why I have to spell everything out. I'm not going to make a scene — I will simply assess and manage. 'We'll see' is the right response.
motivation: Every element of this party must be correct. I need to verify that my instructions were followed precisely.
reflection: I should have specified the lights. Phyllis is not a reliable executor without exhaustive instructions. Noted for next year.
scene_arc: Angela audits the party preparations and interrogates Phyllis about the Christmas lights. Phyllis's choice of small lights draws Angela's visible disapproval. Angela's non-answer leaves Phyllis uncertain and anxious.

title: Michael bursts into party prep and doubles every supply on his own dime | performance
full_dialogue:
Michael: [comes into the conference room in a Santa hat and beard] Merry Christmas! Ho, ho, ho, [points to Ryan] pimp. I'm kidding. What do we got, what do we got? How many plates are we getting?
Angela: Fifty.
Michael: Double it. Double everything. Double ice cream. Double napkins. Double it. On me.
given_circumstances: Michael has received a $3,000 bonus check and is in a generous, expansive mood. The party planning committee, led by Angela, has been working within the established budget. Michael sweeps in to escalate everything.
internal_thoughts: He just doubled everything. He didn't ask. He walked in here in a costume and started overriding three weeks of planning. 'On me' means nothing if it creates logistical chaos.
motivation: I need to maintain control over the party parameters. I have a budget and a plan.
reflection: This is what happens when you let Michael near something organized. He detonates it.
scene_arc: Michael arrives in Santa costume and immediately begins doubling every party supply. He overrides Angela's careful planning with theatrical generosity, declaring everything is on him.

title: Angela asks for help she resents needing | control
full_dialogue: Angela: [having trouble with a plastic tablecloth as Pam stands idly by] Will you help me?
given_circumstances: Angela is setting up the conference room for the Christmas party. Pam is nearby but not actively helping.
internal_thoughts: I am asking Pam for help. I do not enjoy asking for help. She was just standing there. This should not have required a request.
motivation: I need this done correctly and I'm being forced to ask for assistance.
reflection: Fine. It got done. I don't have to like it.
scene_arc: Angela, struggling alone, directly asks Pam for help with a tablecloth.

title: Michael converts Secret Santa to Yankee Swap to chase the iPod | self-deception
characters_involved: Angela, Dwight, Jim, Kevin, Michael, Oscar, Pam
full_dialogue:
Kevin: Should we just keep opening up the presents?
Dwight: We don't do anything until Michael gives us further instructions.
Michael: I got it! We are going to turn Secret Santa into Yankee Swap.
Jim: What is Yankee Swap?
Michael: One person chooses a gift. The next person can either choose a gift or steal that person's gift. If your gift gets stolen, then you can steal somebody else's gift or choose a new gift.
Jim: I thought that was called Nasty Christmas.
Pam: Yeah, we call it White Elephant.
Michael: Well, I call it fun!
Oscar: Why are we doing this?
Michael: Because it's better. Because it's more special.
Angela: It sounds mean.
Michael: Shut it. No, it's not. Okay, just give it a shot.
given_circumstances: Michael has just received the oven mitt and walked out. He has now returned with a new plan. He wants to reorganize the entire gift exchange.
internal_thoughts: He said 'shut it' to me. He is the manager but that was rude. I said it sounds mean because it does sound mean. People chose gifts for specific people. That is now irrelevant.
motivation: I want the party to follow the plan. The plan was Secret Santa.
reflection: He dismantled the party planning committee's work in thirty seconds.
scene_arc: Michael proposes converting Secret Santa into Yankee Swap. Jim and Pam identify it by different names. Oscar questions it. Angela calls it mean. Michael overrides all objections.

title: Angela cries over Michael's protocol violation | control
full_dialogue: Angela: Michael should have asked the party planning committee first. He's not supposed to just spring things on us out of nowhere. [starts to cry]
given_circumstances: Michael has just unilaterally converted Secret Santa to Yankee Swap, bypassing the party planning committee entirely.
internal_thoughts: I cried a little. I am aware I cried a little. It is not just about the party. It is about being disregarded. I planned everything. He walked in and changed it and told me to shut it. I did not deserve that.
motivation: I need people to follow the process I set up.
reflection: I need to keep it together. The party is still happening.
scene_arc: Angela delivers a tearful talking head about being bypassed, showing how deeply the breach of process has affected her.

title: Kelly is horrified by the baby poster and immediately steals the iPod | emotional immediacy
full_dialogue:
Kelly: [unwrapping the poster] Yikes.
Toby: Well, it's for Angela, so ..
Kelly: That's like, the creepiest thing that I've ever seen.
Toby: ...
Dwight: Angela, you're up.
Angela: I'll take the poster. Some people like these.
Kelly: I will steal the iPod.
given_circumstances: The Yankee Swap continues. Toby's gift (the baby-dressed-as-adult poster meant for Angela) has ended up with Kelly through the swap.
internal_thoughts: Kelly said yikes. Kelly does not understand art or beauty. Some people like these. I like these. The poster is mine and I am not going to defend my aesthetic preferences to Kelly.
motivation: I want my poster. It is not creepy. People who say it is creepy have poor taste.
reflection: I have the poster. Kelly's opinion is noted and discarded.
scene_arc: Kelly is horrified by the poster. Toby tries to explain it was for Angela. Angela claims it and defends it. Kelly takes the iPod.

title: Michael brings illegal alcohol to save the party | performance
full_dialogue:
Michael: Uh-oh. Looks like Santa was a little naughty.
Angela: What is that?
Michael: This is Christmas spirit, as in spirits, booze.
Meredith: We can drink?
Toby: We're really not supposed to serve alcohol.
Michael: Zip it, Toby! Just .. I mean, it's a party. Come on. If I can't throw a good party for my employees, then I am a terrible boss. Who wants a drink?
Meredith: Me. Please.
Michael: Go, here we go!
given_circumstances: Michael has returned from the liquor store with fifteen bottles of vodka. Corporate policy prohibits alcohol at work events. Toby is HR.
internal_thoughts: He brought vodka. Fifteen bottles, from the look of it. Toby said something. Michael told Toby to zip it. I am not equipped to stop this. This is going to end badly and it will be in the record.
motivation: I need someone to enforce the policy that Michael is about to violate.
reflection: I am not responsible for what happens next.
scene_arc: Michael enters with alcohol. Angela is alarmed. Meredith is delighted. Toby flags the policy. Michael shuts Toby down and distributes drinks.

title: Michael patrols the party with his camera looking for spectacular moments | performance
full_dialogue:
Michael: Anybody making out in here? [checks hallway] Not yet, give it time. Oh, hey, Ebenezer, boink. [takes picture of Jim] Okay, how's it going in here? [takes picture of Meredith and Kevin]
Ryan: We're running low on cups. Do you want me to just run out and get some?
Angela: There should be some ..
Michael: No, no, no, no. We'll find some, don't leave the party.
given_circumstances: The party is in full swing. Michael has his digital camera. He's touring the party looking for spectacular moments. Ryan is trying to be useful.
internal_thoughts: I was about to tell Ryan where the cups were. Michael preempted with an unnecessary instruction. The cups will need to be found anyway.
motivation: I want the cups situation handled.
reflection: The cups problem isn't solved. I'll handle it.
scene_arc: Michael tours with his camera taking pictures, hoping to manufacture his newsletter cover. Ryan flags a cups shortage. Michael vetoes leaving the party.

title: Angela confronts Kelly about kissing Dwight | control
full_dialogue:
Angela: You behaved very badly tonight.
Kelly: Sorry?
given_circumstances: The party is ending. Angela is confronting Kelly about the kiss with Dwight.
internal_thoughts: She kissed Dwight. That is — it happened in front of people and it was a breach of propriety. I said she behaved badly. She said 'sorry?' like she didn't know what I was talking about. She knows what I'm talking about.
motivation: I need to say this to Kelly. What she did was improper.
reflection: It was said. Kelly knows. Whether she cares is her problem.
scene_arc: Angela tells Kelly she behaved badly. Kelly responds with a surprised 'sorry?'

title: Angela asks Dwight to leave the helm; he refuses in the name of duty | loyalty
full_dialogue:
Dwight: [singing] What do you do with a drunken sailor? What do you do with a drunken sailor? What do you do with a drunken sailor early in the morning?
Angela: Hey, come inside and talk to me.
Dwight: I can't. Do you want us to run aground, woman?!
given_circumstances: Dwight is at the helm, singing to himself. Angela has come to find him.
internal_thoughts: He's singing about drunken sailors and steering a lake boat and won't come inside to talk to me for two minutes. He shouted 'do you want us to run aground, woman.' I am his girlfriend. He is steering a party cruise on a calm lake. This is disproportionate.
motivation: Angela wants a moment of private connection with Dwight and sees the helm as an excuse rather than a real obligation.
reflection: He yelled at me for wanting to talk. I'm going to be fine. But I'm noting this.
scene_arc: Angela tries to get Dwight to leave the helm and spend time with her; Dwight refuses on duty grounds, shouting at her for suggesting it.

title: Darryl calls Angela a bitch and then agrees with her | incidental
full_dialogue:
Darryl: [speaking Spanish] Por favor, tequila seoor.
Angela: I think you've had enough.
Darryl: What you say, bitch? You know, you might be right.
Angela: ...
given_circumstances: Darryl has been drinking. Angela is present.
internal_thoughts: He called me a bitch. And then he agreed with me. I'm choosing to receive the agreement and set aside the other part. He's not wrong that he's had enough.
motivation: Angela is trying to maintain some order and is monitoring the alcohol situation.
reflection: He agreed. That's what matters.
scene_arc: Darryl asks for tequila in Spanish. Angela tells him he's had enough. Darryl calls Angela a bitch and then immediately concedes she's probably right.

title: Dwight flirts with Angela in their secret private way | concealed intimacy
full_dialogue:
Dwight: Huh. Do you like candy?
Angela: It's alright.
Dwight: Cause you're sweeter than candy.
Angela: What is wrong with you? [Dwight pats Angela on the rear and runs away laughing]
Angela: Hey!
given_circumstances: Dwight and Angela are in a secret relationship that the rest of the office does not know about. Dwight approaches Angela at her desk.
internal_thoughts: He's being ridiculous. I love that he's being ridiculous. I have to look offended. I am offended. I'm also not offended at all.
motivation: To maintain the appearance of distance while receiving the interaction she actually wants.
reflection: He ran away laughing. I said hey. This is what we have and it's enough.
scene_arc: Dwight delivers a corny compliment, Angela responds with performative disapproval, Dwight pats her and runs away laughing. The interaction is brief and coded — it reads as harassment to observers but is actually a private shared joke between people who are together.

title: Dwight collapses at his desk and must be taken to the hospital; Pam says goodbye | concussion crisis and unexpected tenderness
characters_involved: Angela, Dwight, Jim, Meredith, Michael, Pam
full_dialogue:
Michael: Uh, finally feel the blood coursing through my foot veins.
Dwight: [hits his head on his desk]  Uh, ugh, ohhhh...
Jim: Uh, ok, I think we need to take him to the hospital because I'm pretty sure he has a concussion.
Michael: Oh, now you feel some compassion for him.
Angela: He needs to go right now, and you're his emergency contact.  I think that you should go with him.
Michael: Why don't you go with him?
Angela: I, barely know him...
Dwight: I want Michael to take me...
Michael: I can't take you, I don't have my car and yours is all vomity.
Meredith: You can take my van!
Michael: Oh, ok, that's, great.  No, I can't drive.  Jim why don't you drive.
Jim: Fine.
Michael: We'll go.  I'm still recovering. So let's just, Ryan, could you get my coat please.
Jim: Slowly, slowly.  Let's just get to the elevator.
Dwight: Choo choo choo choo choo choo...
Jim: What are you doing?  What, stop...
Dwight: Vietnam sounds.
Jim: [Dwight falls onto the couch] Stop, stop, stop, stop.
Dwight: Tired... [Jim grabs spray bottle from planter]
Jim: You can't lay down.
Dwight: Want to take a rake... .
Jim: Wake up. [sprays Dwight]
Dwight: Ahh!
Pam: Dwight, here, let me help you Dwight.
Jim: I'm just gonna get...
Dwight: Ok, Pam, thanks.
Pam: Get up, get up.
Dwight: You're the best.
Pam: Yeah.
Jim: Just keep him awake.
Dwight: It smells like chicken soup.
Pam: I know.
Dwight: I have to go to the hospital.
Dwight: Where we going?
Pam: I just want to say goodbye ok?
Dwight: I'll be back, I mean...
Pam: Yes, I know, but it's gonna be different.
Dwight: Why?
Pam: It's just hard to explain.
Dwight: Aw, Pam, you're adorable [taps her nose]
Pam: Oh my goodness!
Dwight: [giggles]
Pam: Come here.
Dwight: Oh, huggy hugs.
given_circumstances: Dwight has been showing signs of concussion all day since crashing his car into a pole that morning. He has been deteriorating. Angela is his emergency contact. Meredith has a van. Jim will drive.
internal_thoughts: He needs to go now. I'm his emergency contact. I cannot go with him because then people will see my face when I look at him. I barely know him. That's what I said. I barely know him. I hate saying that.
motivation: To ensure Dwight gets care without revealing that she cares.
reflection: He went with Michael. Michael went. That's actually fine. That's what Dwight wanted.
scene_arc: Dwight collapses at his desk from his untreated concussion. The office mobilizes to get him to the hospital. Angela reveals she barely knows him while being his emergency contact. Pam helps get Dwight to his feet, and the two share a genuine, strange goodbye — Pam tells him it will be different after this, meaning the friendship will be acknowledged. Dwight, delirious, taps her nose and they hug.

title: Angela discovers Michael is rubbing butter on his foot and mistakes it for popcorn | accidental horror
full_dialogue: Angela: Ooo! Who made popcorn? [Michael rubbing butter on his foot]
given_circumstances: Michael is in his office applying butter to his foot. Angela walks by and smells something.
internal_thoughts: Popcorn. Someone made popcorn. I want some. Oh.
motivation: To find the popcorn.
reflection: That was not popcorn.
scene_arc: Angela is drawn in by what she thinks is popcorn. The source is Michael's foot butter ritual. The scene ends before she has to process what she's seeing.

title: Dwight assigns cleaning tasks and fixates on Oscar's absence | suspicion
full_dialogue:
Dwight: Meredith, men's room. Make sure you replace the urinal cakes.  They're worn down. Kevin file drawers. Angela kitchen. Oscar dusting. Where is Oscar?
Angela: He's out sick.
Dwight: That's unacceptable.
Angela: I agree it's unacceptable. [longing look]
Kevin: Whhh... What are you guys doing?
given_circumstances: Dwight is organizing spring cleaning assignments for the office. Oscar is absent, which immediately stands out to him.
internal_thoughts: Dwight notices what matters. I notice that Dwight notices. Kevin has no idea what is going on, which feels normal.
motivation: To align herself with Dwight's seriousness and shared standards.
reflection: Shared standards are attractive. Chaos is not.
scene_arc: Dwight hands out tasks and notices Oscar is missing. Angela validates Dwight's concern while Kevin remains confused by the whole operation.

title: Michael gets caught trying to expense Hooters | exposure
full_dialogue:
Michael: [Michael messes up hair to look like Jim's] Expenses.
Kevin: Michael is that a wig?
Michael: No. It's... I wear it like that sometimes. Is that a wig?
Kevin: No.
Angela: This is from Hooters.
Michael: Yeah, it's a business lunch.
Angela: Did Toby approve this?
Michael: No he did not. I don't need his permission.
Toby: You just got your corporate credit card back. Do you really want me to take it away again?
given_circumstances: Michael is trying to classify lunch with Jim as a business expense. Other people notice before the paperwork can quietly pass through.
internal_thoughts: This evidence is obvious and tacky. Somebody has to name what it is.
motivation: To call out something improper when she sees it.
reflection: If something is tacky, I will say so.
scene_arc: Kevin questions Michael's appearance, Angela notices the Hooters receipt, and Toby reminds Michael that his credit-card privileges are already fragile. Michael's attempt to normalize the lunch collapses.

title: Phyllis asks Angela to pick Jim or Roy and Angela chooses Roy | gossip
full_dialogue:
Phyllis: Angela who would you choose Jim or Roy?
Angela: It's nobody's business, Phyllis. Roy.
given_circumstances: The office has started gossiping after Michael let Jim's secret slip. Phyllis pulls Angela into the conversation.
internal_thoughts: It is nobody's business. Roy. There, done.
motivation: To signal judgment while still giving an answer.
reflection: Giving one answer does not mean I joined the talk. Probably.
scene_arc: Phyllis asks Angela whom she would choose between Jim and Roy. Angela resists the gossip on principle and then still gives a one-word answer.

title: Michael announces a sales contest with $83; decides to compete himself | competition created and immediately corrupted
full_dialogue:
Michael: Hi, guys.
Angela: We haven't finished getting things in order from your last visit.
Michael: I'm just walking around.
Angela: Were you?
Michael: Well, yeah.
Oscar: It's just that we're really swamped over here, Michael.
Michael: Oh, and I'm not?  Why would you say that? Because I'm having fun?  You guys just are workin' for the weekend, aren't you?  I'm workin' for the week.  Sales team, listen to me.  This is what we're gonna do.  I'm gonna up the ante a little bit literally.  Right here, I'm gonna put a crisp one hundred dollar bill.  The person with the most sales at the end of the day gets to keep the cash.  Sound good?
Dwight: Yeah!
Michael: [counting cash] Seventy, eighty, one, two three.  Eighty-three dollars.  Still a lotta money and I'm going to ... [moves money after seeing workmen walk by] ... I'm gonna leave it right over here where everybody can see it.  I will be taking Jim's clients today because he is not here and out of sight, out of the contest.  Let's see who winds up with the cash, shall we?
Phyllis: You're gonna compete against us?
Michael: Oh, it is on, Phyllis, it is so on!
Dwight: It is so on!
Michael: God, this is gonna be fun.
Dwight: Michael is gonna wipe the floor with us!
given_circumstances: Michael is in the accounting area and wants to create motivation. He proposes a sales contest with cash.
internal_thoughts: He's back. He just raided us. He's walking around like that's fine. I'm telling him we haven't recovered. He doesn't care.
motivation: To recover her area from the previous raid and minimize further disruption.
reflection: He ignored me and started a contest. Now we have a contest.
scene_arc: Michael announces a $100 prize contest that turns out to be $83. He also decides to compete in the contest himself and claim Jim's clients for the day. Phyllis notes the obvious conflict. Michael confirms he is competing. Dwight helpfully declares Michael will win.

title: Angela says obviously Kevin did it | swift certain blame
full_dialogue: Angela: Obviously, this was Kevin. This is his sense of humor exactly.
given_circumstances: Angela addresses the camera about the carpet incident.
internal_thoughts: Kevin. It's Kevin. This is what Kevin finds funny. Obviously.
motivation: To name the guilty party.
reflection: Kevin did it. I'm certain.
scene_arc: Angela delivers her verdict on the carpet incident: Kevin, obviously.

title: Angela tells Dwight he could out-sell Michael any day | private validation
full_dialogue:
Dwight: Okay, look, I know what you're gonna say. That I'm not standing up for myself. But you know what? It's complicated and I really don't appreciate all the badgering.
Angela: You could out-sell Michael any day.
given_circumstances: Dwight has been coaching-managed and sale-stolen by Michael. Angela knows this.
internal_thoughts: He's defending himself before I've said anything. He's right — it's complicated. I know the Michael thing. I'm not going to badger him. He needs to hear this: he's better than Michael. He is.
motivation: To give Dwight the thing he actually needs, not the critique she could give.
reflection: I said the true thing. He needed to hear it.
scene_arc: Dwight anticipates criticism from Angela for not defending himself against Michael. Angela instead delivers the exact validation he needed: he could out-sell Michael any day. She says it plainly.

title: Jan runs a 'what you're good at' exercise; Meredith almost says alcoholic; Phyllis's spreadsheets are challenged by Angela | skills circle
full_dialogue:
Jan: Why don't we go around the table and all say something that we know we're good at. I will start. I am good at public speaking.
Meredith: Hi. I'm Meredith and I'm an alch... good at supplier relations.
Jan: Great. Phyllis?
Phyllis: I'm good at computer stuff, emails, spreadsheets, all that.
Angela: [disbelieving] Really?
Phyllis: I don't know. I thought that I wasn't going to be asked that...
Jan: No. Okay. Stop. Go on...
Angela: I've seen some of your spreadsheets.
Phyllis: Really? I thought they were pretty...
given_circumstances: The women's seminar is running a self-affirmation exercise.
internal_thoughts: I've seen her spreadsheets. The claim does not match the evidence.
motivation: To maintain accuracy.
reflection: I said what I know to be true.
scene_arc: Jan models the exercise. Meredith nearly identifies herself as an alcoholic before pivoting to supplier relations. Phyllis claims spreadsheet skills; Angela immediately challenges this based on firsthand experience.

title: Angela says she gains nothing from the seminar and that Jan aspires to be a whore based on her outfit | judgment delivered privately
full_dialogue: Angela: I'm not gaining anything from this seminar. I'm a professional woman. The head of accounting. I'm in the healthiest relationship of my life. I just think it's insulting that Jan thinks we need this. And, apparently, judging from her outfit, Jan aspires to be a whore.
given_circumstances: Angela addresses the camera during the clothing discussion.
internal_thoughts: I don't need this. Head of accounting. Healthiest relationship of my life — Dwight. Insulting that Jan thinks we need this. Her outfit. If she's teaching dress-for-the-job-you-want, that outfit says something specific.
motivation: To register her objection to the seminar and evaluate Jan's clothing credibility.
reflection: The outfit assessment was accurate based on the principle she just taught.
scene_arc: Angela dismisses the seminar as unnecessary for someone at her level, judges Jan's outfit harshly, and closes with the healthiest relationship of her life — a statement that must be read in the context of the secret Dwight relationship.

title: Angela asks if Jan is married; Jan says divorced; Kelly says that's probably why she hooked up with Michael; Jan redirects to careers; Kelly says how can someone so beautiful be so sad | the women see Jan clearly
full_dialogue:
Angela: Are you married?
Jan: I'm divorced.
Phyllis: That must have been hard.
Jan: It was. Yes.
Kelly: You were probably feeling really depressed and sad and that's why you did that thing with Michael.
Jan: I think you should all spend a little more time thinking about your careers and less time on personal stuff.
Phyllis: Mmmm, I think we're all okay with the balance we've struck.
Angela: At least you don't have kids. You have no kids, right? Thank God.
Jan: Okay. Let's take five. I think we can all use five.
Kelly: How can someone so beautiful be so sad?
given_circumstances: The seminar has moved into personal territory.
internal_thoughts: At least no kids. That's a genuine consolation.
motivation: To note the silver lining of Jan's childlessness.
reflection: No kids. Good.
scene_arc: Jan discloses her divorce. Phyllis offers sympathy. Kelly immediately connects it to Michael. Jan redirects to careers. Phyllis defends the group's current balance. Angela says at least no kids. Jan calls a five-minute break. Kelly closes with a sincere observation about Jan's beauty and sadness.

title: Dwight finds a bobblehead of himself on his desk; realizes it's from Angela; reacts with pure joy | secret love received
full_dialogue:
Dwight: What's this? What is this?
Jim: I dunno, it's on
Dwight: Yeah, but who put it here? And for what purpose?
Jim: It was there when I sat down.
Dwight: [opens box and reads card] Happy Valentine's Day. [pulls out bobble head] It's me. I'm the bobble head. Yes! [Angela smirks in background] Ahh!
given_circumstances: Dwight arrives at his desk to find a small gift box. Jim is nearby. Angela is in the background.
internal_thoughts: He opened it. He said yes. He knew immediately. I'm smirking — I need to stop smirking. I'm containing it. He's so happy.
motivation: To watch him receive it without anyone seeing her watch.
reflection: He said yes. That was the right gift. I knew it would be.
scene_arc: Dwight finds a bobblehead of himself, reads the card, and erupts in genuine delight. Angela watches from the background with a contained smirk. Jim has no idea what's happening.

title: Dwight tells Angela someone got him the best gift he's ever received; Angela pretends not to know anything; promises she'll get something too | covert gift loop
full_dialogue:
Dwight: Hello Angela. Did you hear, somebody rocked the house and got me the best present I've ever gotten.
Angela: Really? I wouldn't know anything about that, but I'm glad you enjoyed it.
Dwight: Oh I did. I did.
Angela: I didn't get anything for Valentine's Day.
Dwight: Oh, I bet you will before the day is over.
Angela: Really? Well, I hope I do.
given_circumstances: Dwight approaches Angela's desk. He has just received the bobblehead. Angela gave it to him but the relationship is secret.
internal_thoughts: He knows it was me. He's saying it without saying it. I'm saying I wouldn't know anything. I mentioned I didn't get anything — that was deliberate. He said I will before the day is over. He means it.
motivation: To maintain plausible deniability while receiving confirmation that he loved it — and to plant the expectation of reciprocation.
reflection: He promised I'll get something. He never breaks a promise like that.
scene_arc: Dwight announces the bobblehead to Angela in a way that lets her know he knows. Angela maintains deniability while noting she got nothing. Dwight promises she will before the day ends. The exchange is fully coded.

title: Oscar receives a Valentine's plant from his mom; Angela gets nothing; Pam delivers it and says 'join the club' | quiet inventory of the unloved
characters_involved: Angela, Delivery man, Kevin, Oscar, Pam
full_dialogue:
Delivery man: [with flowers] Can you sign?
Pam: [delivering plant to Oscar's desk] Oscar.
Angela: Nothing for me?
Pam: [walks away] Join the club.
Kevin: Whose it from? [to Oscar]
Oscar: My mom. [puts card in pocket]
given_circumstances: Another delivery arrives at the office. This one is a plant addressed to Oscar.
internal_thoughts: Nothing for me. Pam said join the club. Dwight promised before the day was over. It's not over.
motivation: To check whether anything has arrived for her.
reflection: Pam said join the club. Dwight said before the day is over. I'm waiting.
scene_arc: Pam delivers a plant to Oscar. Angela asks if there's nothing for her. Pam says join the club and walks away. Oscar's plant is from his mom, which he quietly pockets.

title: Dwight delivers cascading false bad news to demonstrate his public speaking; Michael confesses it was all fake; Stanley asks about the bonuses | Dwight's failed lesson attempt
characters_involved: Angela, Dwight, Kelly, Michael, Oscar, Pam, Phyllis, Stanley
full_dialogue:
Stanley: Go ahead. Get the wallpaper. Wallpaper the ceiling if you want. Call Terri and tell her she...
Phyllis: It's unbelievable!
Dwight: Excuse me! May I have your attention please? There has been an accident on 84 West. Cars have skidded off the road into the safety railing. Several cars have flipped. There is broken glass everywhere. Several people are injured.
Pam: Do we know anyone who was in the accident?
Dwight: Brad Pitt. Also there will be no bonuses.
Stanley: Why would this affect our bonuses?
Dwight: They are unrelated.
Kelly: Is Brad okay?
Dwight: He will never act again. Also, this branch is closing.
Oscar: What the hell is going on here?
Angela: Are we out of jobs?
Kelly: This is karma because of what he did to Jennifer Aniston.
Michael: He's kidding. Dwight was kidding and I don't know why because it wasn't funny... and it was just horrible.
Stanley: Michael?
Stanley: You said we were getting bonuses.
Michael: All right. Everybody in the conference room now. Let's go. Let's do it.
Stanley: Cancel wallpaper.
given_circumstances: Dwight is trying to demonstrate he's learned Michael's lesson. He begins announcing bad news to the office.
internal_thoughts: Are we out of jobs. He said yes. Conference room.
motivation: To understand her employment status.
reflection: We're going to the conference room.
scene_arc: Dwight attempts to demonstrate Michael's 'say anything' lesson by announcing a highway crash, Brad Pitt's career ending, the bonus cancellation, and branch closure — all unrelated. Michael distances himself. Stanley asks about the bonuses. The conference room is summoned. Stanley cancels the wallpaper.

title: Michael tells Dwight the grim reaper is here; Angela wishes Dwight the very best of luck | the send-off
full_dialogue:
Michael: It's time, Dwight. The grim reaper is here.
Angela: The very best of luck to you, Dwight.
Dwight: Thank you, Angela.
given_circumstances: It is time to go to the convention.
internal_thoughts: Very best of luck. I mean it. I'm saying it formally because we can't say anything else here. Very best of luck.
motivation: To send Dwight with something real.
reflection: I meant it. He thanked me.
scene_arc: Michael invokes death. Angela gives a formal blessing. Dwight thanks her.

title: Angela coughs and says she might go home sick; Kevin notes he's never seen her take a sick day; Angela fires back | the foreshadowing
full_dialogue:
Angela: [coughs] [sniffles] I am just feeling under the weather. And... I think that I will go home and rest.
Kevin: I've never, ever seen you take a sick day.
Angela: Well, I've seen you take enough for the both of us.
given_circumstances: Angela is not feeling well — or appearing not to.
internal_thoughts: Under the weather. I'll go home and rest. Kevin said he's never seen me take a sick day. I said he's taken enough for both of us. I need to go. Dwight is going to give a speech.
motivation: To leave the office for reasons she can't state.
reflection: I need to be there. I said under the weather. I'm going.
scene_arc: Angela claims illness and is going home. Kevin notes the unprecedented nature of it. Angela deflects with Kevin's record.

title: Sasha offers to help with setup; Angela declines; Kelly says she can't wait to get pregnant; Angela says she'd want two small well-behaved boys | contrasting visions of motherhood
episode_title: Take Your Daughter to Work Day
full_dialogue:
Angela: Can you put that down there?
Kelly: Yep. [spreads tablecloth]
Toby: [to Sasha] Okay, tell them what you wanted to say.
Sasha: Do you need any help?
Angela: No. Thanks. We'd... have to explain everything, it's probably just easier if we do it ourselves.
Toby: Alright, I wasn't expecting that. Let's uh... let's go draw.
Kelly: Oh my God, she is so cute, I want to die. Don't you just love kids, Angela?
Angela: I guess I wouldn't mind a pair of small, well-behaved boys.
Kelly: God I cannot wait to get pregnant and have babies!
given_circumstances: Angela and Kelly are setting up something. Toby has brought Sasha over to help.
internal_thoughts: The child offered to help. Teaching her would be slower than doing it. I said no, thank you. Kelly is asking if I love kids. A pair of small well-behaved boys. That is what I would want. I made eye contact with Dwight. He knows what I mean.
motivation: To do the task correctly and specify her parenting preferences when asked.
reflection: Two boys. Well-behaved. Small. That's the specification.
scene_arc: Toby brings Sasha to help. Angela declines the help efficiently, sending them away. Kelly gushes. Angela articulates her ideal children: a pair, small, well-behaved, male.

title: Angela says she hopes her future mate has the same strict disciplinarian qualities as her father and makes eye contact with Dwight | private signal sent
episode_title: Take Your Daughter to Work Day
full_dialogue: Angela: You know, I never misbehaved in front of my father because he was a very strict disciplinarian. I can only hope my mate has some of those same qualities [makes eye contact with Dwight].
given_circumstances: Angela addresses the camera about her upbringing and future family.
internal_thoughts: My father was strict. I admired that. My mate should be that. I'm looking at Dwight while saying this. He knows.
motivation: To communicate her expectations to Dwight in a format the camera records but no one can accuse her of stating directly.
reflection: I said it on camera. He saw the look. That's a message delivered.
scene_arc: Angela describes her father's disciplinary style approvingly and signals to Dwight — via eye contact — that this is the standard she holds for a partner.

title: Angela orders pizza; refuses pepperoni and extra cheese; threatens to teach children about obesity | dietary control extended to others
episode_title: Take Your Daughter to Work Day
full_dialogue:
Angela: Okay. I think five plain and one veggie should be fine.
Phyllis: How about pepperoni?
Ryan: I like extra cheese.
Angela: Absolutely not.
Pam: I like extra cheese, too.
Angela: Fine. The first lesson we'll teach children will be about obesity.
Ryan: Thank you.
given_circumstances: The group is ordering pizza for the children. Angela is making the decisions.
internal_thoughts: Plain and veggie. Phyllis wants pepperoni. No. Ryan wants extra cheese. No. Pam too. Fine — obesity lesson. That's the consequence.
motivation: To ensure the children are fed appropriately and that she maintains control over the order.
reflection: Plain and veggie. The lesson about obesity is a real option I have.
scene_arc: Angela orders plain and veggie. Phyllis requests pepperoni — no. Ryan requests extra cheese — absolutely not. Pam agrees with Ryan — Angela threatens to teach obesity as the lesson. Ryan thanks her.

title: Kevin asks Angela if he can be head accountant just for today; Angela refuses on the grounds that Abby is a bright girl | authority preserved against a child
episode_title: Take Your Daughter to Work Day
full_dialogue:
Abby: I'm gonna go read my book now.
Kevin: [whispering] Angela. Is it okay if I'm the head accountant today?
Angela: I'm the head accountant.
Kevin: Yeah, but can I say that I'm it, just for today?
Angela: I can't set that kind of precedent. She's a bright girl. She'd see right through that.
given_circumstances: Abby has said she's going to read her book. Kevin asks Angela a favor.
internal_thoughts: I'm the head accountant. Kevin wants to say he is just for today. The precedent is impossible. Abby is a bright girl. She would know.
motivation: To maintain accurate organizational structure and protect Abby from misinformation.
reflection: I can't set that precedent. That's the correct position.
scene_arc: Kevin requests to be named head accountant for the day, just to say it. Angela refuses on the grounds that Abby is bright enough to see through the fiction.

title: The party committee collides with Dwight's surprise demands | control
full_dialogue:
Phyllis: When should we bring out the cake, one or one thirty?
Pam: One's good.
Angela: One thirty. [Pam yawns] I'm sorry, are we boring you?
Dwight: Party planning committee, listen up. Michael would like trick candles for his birthday cake, so make that a priority.
Phyllis: Where do we get those?
Dwight: Not my problem. Here is a list of things that Michael would like to be surprised by. [hands list to Pam]
Pam: Michael wants a strippergram?
Dwight: Yes, but he doesn't want to know when, or whom.
Angela: No. This is a closed door meeting.
given_circumstances: The party planning committee is trying to settle practical timing for Michael's birthday celebration. Dwight inserts himself with a list of Michael's requests and treats the matter as urgent.
internal_thoughts: This is my committee, not Dwight's circus. A strippergram is inappropriate, and Pam yawning in a meeting is also inappropriate. If I do not impose standards, Michael's birthday will become vulgar and disorganized.
motivation: protect the party planning committee's authority and moral boundaries
reflection: I had to close the door because people were forgetting what a committee is for. Order and decency do not happen by accident here.
scene_arc: Phyllis, Pam, and Angela debate timing while Dwight demands trick candles and relays increasingly inappropriate surprise ideas. Angela shuts down the discussion by asserting committee authority.

title: Oscar comforts Kevin while Angela refuses easy reassurance | blunt care
full_dialogue:
Oscar: Skin cancer is treatable.
Kevin: Right.
Oscar: It's going to be okay.
Angela: You don't know it's going to be okay. Don't give him false hope. ...It's probably nothing, though.
given_circumstances: Kevin is still waiting for results and coworkers are trying to respond. Oscar chooses reassurance, while Angela is uncomfortable with promises no one can guarantee.
internal_thoughts: Oscar should not promise outcomes he cannot know. False hope is sloppy, and sloppy comfort can be cruel. Still, I know Kevin is frightened, so I add that it is probably nothing because I am not heartless.
motivation: correct false reassurance while offering a restrained version of comfort
reflection: I was honest, maybe too honest. But in this office, someone has to say what is true instead of what feels pleasant.
scene_arc: Oscar tells Kevin the condition is treatable and that he will be okay. Angela interrupts to reject false hope, then softens only slightly by saying it is probably nothing.

title: Michael offers identical subs and feels rejected by the room | generosity
full_dialogue:
Dwight: Here they come.
Michael: Get in here... everybody.
Dwight: Come and get it!
Michael: Birthday party subs! My gift to you.
Oscar: What is this?
Dwight: Uh, bologna, tomato and ketchup.
Michael: The best.
Stanley: These are all the same?
Michael: Yes.
Angela: Bologna? I don't eat bologna.
Michael: Well, then just have the tomato and ketchup. Still good.
Michael: Just the bread, it's fresh baked.
Michael: Mm-kay. Get whatever you want. [under breath] And choke on it.
given_circumstances: Michael and Dwight present the birthday food to the office. Michael frames the food as a gift from himself to others and expects appreciation.
internal_thoughts: Bologna is unacceptable, and ketchup does not rescue it. Michael keeps narrowing the offer down to tomato, bread, anything, but I am not obligated to eat something because it is his birthday. Saying no is the cleanest available option.
motivation: refuse food that violates my standards and resist Michael's pressure
reflection: I maintained a standard. Michael responded with resentment because he treats refusal as personal disloyalty.
scene_arc: The staff discovers that the sandwiches are all the same unappealing combination. Angela refuses every compromise Michael offers, and Michael turns wounded generosity into a muttered insult.

title: Dwight and Angela guard the cake while revealing their private tension | secrecy
full_dialogue:
Dwight: Hey temp, you know uh, we still got five feet of sandwich left [pulls ice cream cake out of freezer].
Ryan: [making peanut butter and jelly sandwich] Someone ate three feet of that thing?
Dwight: Hell, yeah. Save room for ice cream cake.
Angela: [grabs cake] Oh. Thank you.
Dwight: Oh. I got it.
Angela: What are--- it's... the party planning committee.
Dwight: [whispering] This is the most important day of the year. I can't risk anything.
Angela: Fine.
Dwight: What about that meeting... later... to discuss finances?
Angela: Yes... [whispering] but don't expect any cookie.
Dwight: [whispering] But what if i'm hungry?
Angela: [whispering]
given_circumstances: Dwight is still trying to control Michael's birthday celebration, while Angela protects the party planning committee's authority. Ryan is nearby enough to witness the tension.
internal_thoughts: The ice cream cake belongs under committee control, not Dwight's anxious hands. He is useful, but he also turns Michael's birthday into a military operation. Our whispered finances meeting is private, and I will not let him confuse that with getting cookies.
motivation: reclaim party planning authority and keep the secret Angela-Dwight dynamic controlled
reflection: I took the cake because boundaries matter. With Dwight, I have to manage both the party and the private undercurrent.
scene_arc: Dwight retrieves the ice cream cake and Angela takes it back in the name of the committee. Their whispered exchange shifts from logistics into coded intimacy before the scene cuts away.

title: Kevin's fear interrupts Michael's birthday song | displacement
characters_involved: Angela, Dwight, Kevin, Michael, Office Staff
full_dialogue:
Office Staff: [singing] Happy birthday dear Michael, [Michael joins in, Kevin's phone rings] Happy birthday... [everyone but Dwight stops] ...tooo youuuu.
Kevin: Hello? Hey.
Michael: Kevin? Respect the birthday please.
Kevin: No, um, no not yet. I will. Bye. [hangs up] It was just Stacy.
Michael: Are you done? ...Good. Okay.
Dwight: Here we go. Make a wish.
Michael: Uhhh... blow out the candle. Okay. Mmmm... [blows out the candles]
Dwight: Yaoo yay! [claps]
Michael: ... I asked for trick candles.
Dwight: Pam was supposed to get 'em.
Michael: Okay. Well, when she comes back we'll do it again. [notices Meredith hugging Kevin] Hello, what about the birthday boy? Haven't had a hug all day.
Angela: No one cares about your birthday. Kevin's waiting to hear if he has skin cancer.
Michael: ... Aww, that... sucks, great. ... Wow, that's good timing. That's... that's, sorry, that's terrible. Terrible news. That's terrible... terrible news for both of us [takes cake into office and slams the door].
given_circumstances: The office has gathered to sing to Michael and present the cake. Kevin is still waiting for his medical update, making the celebratory mood fragile.
internal_thoughts: Michael complaining about hugs while Kevin is waiting on skin cancer is indecent. Someone has to puncture the birthday fantasy and say what the room is actually feeling. I do not care if it sounds harsh; it is morally correct.
motivation: force Michael to recognize Kevin's fear instead of centering his birthday
reflection: I said the thing everyone was avoiding. Michael needed to hear that Kevin's health mattered more than cake.
scene_arc: Kevin takes a call during the song, upsetting Michael, and the candles are not the trick candles Michael requested. Angela finally tells Michael that people are focused on Kevin's possible cancer, and Michael retreats angrily with the cake.

title: Dwight tells Angela he knows she's innocent but has to interview her to maintain appearances; she smiles | secret relationship as institutional cover
full_dialogue:
Dwight: I know you're innocent, but I can't look like I'm treating you any differently.
Angela: I understand.
Dwight: Where were you yesterday after work?
Angela: [smiles knowingly]
given_circumstances: Dwight is interviewing Angela as part of his investigation.
internal_thoughts: He said he knows I'm innocent. He asked where I was. I'm smiling. He knows where I was.
motivation: To be formally interviewed and smile at the right question.
reflection: He knows.
scene_arc: Dwight tells Angela he knows she's innocent but must interview her for appearance's sake. She understands. He asks where she was. She smiles.

title: Dwight asks Kevin about Rogaine; asks Angela about prescription drugs; Angela gives Dwight a look and he says good | coded exemption issued
full_dialogue:
Dwight: Kevin, what prescription drugs are you taking, besides Rogaine?
Kevin: I'm not taking Rogaine.
Dwight: Angela, what about you?
Angela: I don't take any prescription drugs.
Dwight: You're not on
Angela: [Gives Dwight a knowing look]
Dwight: Good.
given_circumstances: Dwight is going through prescription drug disclosures.
internal_thoughts: He started to ask something specific. I gave him the look. He said good.
motivation: To stop Dwight's question before it gets specific.
reflection: The look worked.
scene_arc: Dwight asks Kevin about Rogaine — Kevin denies it. Dwight asks Angela about prescription drugs. She denies any. He starts to ask a specific follow-up. She gives him a look. He says good.

title: Angela tells Dwight she won't give permission to give Michael his urine; Dwight says he knows that | permission sought and correctly denied
full_dialogue:
Angela: Do you want to give Michael your urine?
Dwight: I want him to have all the urine he needs.
Angela: You're not going to get my permission on this.
Dwight: I know that. Don't you think I know that?
given_circumstances: Dwight has apparently consulted Angela about the urine situation.
internal_thoughts: He wants to give Michael his urine to pass a drug test. That's illegal. I won't sanction it. I said so.
motivation: To be clear about her position on illegal activity.
reflection: I gave him no permission. That was correct.
scene_arc: Angela asks Dwight's intent. Dwight expresses wanting Michael to have all the urine he needs — a statement of loyalty. Angela refuses permission. Dwight says he knows.

title: Angela looks forward to drug testing because she used to get a runner's high so now she swims; Meredith says she's 100% drug-free, there are plenty of other ways to have fun | parallel self-characterizations
full_dialogue:
Angela: I look forward to drug testing. I used to get a runner's high, which is why now I swim.
Meredith: Why would I be worried? I'm not a drug user. I would never abuse my body like that. I mean, they are plenty of other ways to have fun. My body is 100% drug-free.
given_circumstances: Angela and Meredith address the camera about the drug test.
internal_thoughts: Runner's high is a high. I swim now. No highs. I look forward to the test.
motivation: To establish her total abstinence from altered states, including legal ones.
reflection: Swimming produces no high. That is correct.
scene_arc: Angela explains she avoids even runner's highs — hence swimming. Meredith clarifies her body is 100% drug-free, while noting there are plenty of other ways to have fun.

title: Angela realizes she may not be invited to Pam's wedding | social exclusion
full_dialogue:
Phyllis: Oh I got the 'Save The Date'.
Pam: Yeah?
Phyllis: Yeah, pretty stationery.
Pam: Oh, thanks!
Angela: I didn't get mine yet.
Pam: Uh...
given_circumstances: Pam has sent Save The Date cards for her wedding, and some coworkers have already received them. Angela has a history of judging Pam, which makes the invitation list emotionally charged.
internal_thoughts: Phyllis got the card, which means this was not an accident of timing. Pam is trying to act sweet while quietly deciding I am not worthy of her wedding. I will not beg for an invitation, but I will make the omission visible.
motivation: force Pam to acknowledge that Angela has been excluded
reflection: Pam's silence told me enough. If she wants to be tacky and selective, I can still judge the invitation from a position of standards.
scene_arc: Phyllis warmly acknowledges receiving the card, and Pam responds with relief. Angela notices that she has not received one and puts Pam on the spot, ending the exchange in awkward silence.

title: Michael teaches conflict styles and invents win-win-win | managerial fantasy
full_dialogue:
Michael: [holding up a binder] A mediator's tool chest. Okay, well, before we get started, you should know that are 5 different styles of conflict.[in a comedic voice] My Shaolin temple style defeats your monkey style.
Angela: Can we go? I have a lot of work to do.
Michael: No. Okay, this is important. The first style is lose/lose.
Oscar: What's the next one?
Michael: Just hold on, please! Okay, if we do lose/lose, neither of you gets what you want. Do you understand? You... you would both lose. Now I need to ask you, do you want to pursue a lose/lose negotiation?
Angela: Can we just skip to whatever number 5 is - win/win or whatever?
Michael: Win/Win is number four and number five is win/win/win. The important difference here is with win/win/win, we all win. Me too. I win for having successfully mediated a conflict at work.
given_circumstances: Michael has taken over Angela and Oscar's poster dispute with a mediator's binder. He wants the process to demonstrate his competence, not merely solve the employees' problem.
internal_thoughts: People here confuse rudeness with honesty and vulgarity with personality. I maintain standards because someone has to. If others feel judged, that may be because they are behaving in ways worth judging.
motivation: preserve standards, privacy, and moral authority in a messy office
reflection: The office is too casual with private matters and too careless with taste. I will keep my composure because standards are not decided by majority vote.
scene_arc: Michael explains conflict-resolution styles in a martial-arts voice and insists on working through the framework. Angela wants to skip ahead, and Michael reveals that his preferred outcome is win/win/win because he gets to win as mediator too.

title: Michael mediates the baby poster dispute with a t-shirt solution | forced harmony
full_dialogue:
Michael: [in front of poster] Okay, since this is the disputed poster. Now, one at a time, I want you to express your feelings using 'I' emotion language and no judging or 'you' statements.
Angela: I got this poster for Christmas, and I feel I want to see it everyday. It makes me feel like the babies are the true artists, and God has a really cute sense of humor.
Michael: Come on, seriously, that?
Oscar: I don't like looking at it. It's creepy, and in bad taste, and it's just offensive to me. It makes me think of the horrible, frigid stage mothers who force the babies into it. It's kitsch. It's the opposite of art. It destroys art. It destroys souls. This is so much more offensive to me than hardcore porno. I'm talking about the...
Michael: Okay, okay. Stop, stop, stop! Let's see if we can just brainstorm and find some creative alternatives that are win/win.
Pam: Win!
Michael: Yes. Thank you, Pam. How about Angela makes the poster into a t-shirt, which Oscar wears. That way, he can never see it and whenever she looks at Oscar, she can see it. Win/win/win.
Oscar: No.
Angela: That's... no...
Michael: Okay... well, brainstorm. Own the solution.
Angela: How about, I leave it up?
Oscar: How 'bout, she takes it down?
Pam: How about, Angela can keep it up on Tuesdays and Thursdays?
Michael: Okay, that is called a compromise. And it is style 3. And it is not ideal. To sum up, win/win - make the poster into a t-shirt, win/lose - take the poster down, compromise - Tuesdays and Thursdays. And the answer is... make the poster into a t-shirt! Win/win.
Pam: Win.
Oscar: Fine.
Angela: But, it...
Michael: [claps his hands twice] It is done!
Pam: Win
given_circumstances: Angela wants her poster visible, Oscar finds it deeply offensive, and Michael is determined to produce a theatrical resolution. Pam is present as a helper and observer, giving Michael small encouragements while the employees resist.
internal_thoughts: The poster is wholesome, and Oscar is being dramatic because he enjoys feeling superior. I should not have to justify liking babies dressed nicely. Michael's process is ridiculous, but if it keeps the poster in sight, I can tolerate some indignity.
motivation: keep the poster visible and resist having her taste judged by Oscar or Michael
reflection: The t-shirt idea was humiliating and impractical, but Oscar had to concede something. I still believe the poster deserved respect.
scene_arc: Angela and Oscar each state their positions, and Michael dismisses or truncates the emotional substance. He proposes that Oscar wear the poster as a t-shirt, rejects Pam's compromise as inferior, and declares the t-shirt idea done despite weak consent.

title: Pam hand-delivers Angela a Save The Date | reluctant inclusion
full_dialogue:
Pam: Hey, Angela.[hands her a Save The Date card] I didn't have your zip code.
Angela: Oh. Thanks.
given_circumstances: Pam has been deciding who should receive wedding Save The Dates and has avoided Angela because of past judgment. Angela's visible exclusion made the workplace awkward enough that Pam now tries to smooth it over.
internal_thoughts: People here confuse rudeness with honesty and vulgarity with personality. I maintain standards because someone has to. If others feel judged, that may be because they are behaving in ways worth judging.
motivation: preserve standards, privacy, and moral authority in a messy office
reflection: The office is too casual with private matters and too careless with taste. I will keep my composure because standards are not decided by majority vote.
scene_arc: Pam gives Angela a Save The Date and explains that she did not have Angela's zip code. Angela accepts it with restrained politeness.

title: Angela evaluates Pam's Save The Date after receiving it late | wounded superiority
full_dialogue: Angela: It was hand delivered. But, I did get a Save The Date after all. It's not my taste.
given_circumstances: Angela has now received a Save The Date, but the manner of delivery makes clear that she was not included naturally. She protects her pride by turning the invitation into an object of judgment.
internal_thoughts: People here confuse rudeness with honesty and vulgarity with personality. I maintain standards because someone has to. If others feel judged, that may be because they are behaving in ways worth judging.
motivation: preserve standards, privacy, and moral authority in a messy office
reflection: The office is too casual with private matters and too careless with taste. I will keep my composure because standards are not decided by majority vote.
scene_arc: Angela tells the camera that the card was hand-delivered but counts as received. She undercuts the moment by saying it is not to her taste.

title: Michael opens the complaint files to the whole office | public exposure
full_dialogue:
Michael: How many of you have at one time gone to Toby to complain about another employee? [looks at all the employees, most of whom raise their hands] And... did you get what you wanted, or were you merely listened to, you forget about your problem, and you move on? [employees mumble 'merely listen to and forgotten...' ] That is outrageous! I love this place... and it pains me to see all of the negativity festering... [sighs] Okay, today we are going to get everything out of these files and into the open, where it can be resolved. Alright, how about the Phyllis/Angela dispute?
Angela: You already did me.
Michael: That's what she said. [Jim mouths these words along with Michael] The thing is, Angela... you are in here an awful lot. You have complained about everybody in the office, except Dwight, which is odd because everyone else has had run ins with Dwight. Toby, by the way, what does 'redacted' mean? There is a file full of complaints in here marked 'redacted'... ?
Toby: Yeah, it just means whoever complained came to me later and withdrew it, so I took their name off.
Michael: Oh, ok. There were a bunch of complaints about Dwight that were put in the redacted file about six months ago.
Dwight: Whoa.. wha... wait. If someone has a problem with me, why would they withdraw it six months ago?
Pam: [notices Angela's intense concern] Um... let's move on. I volunteer. Did anyone have a problem with me?
Michael: All right, Pamela. Come on down! Let's do it! And [looks through the file]... okay. Just one complaint. Actually, it has been withdrawn. So that is no help to us. Next.
Pam: Wait, what did it say?
Michael: Uh... [reading]'Does she have to plan her wedding on office time? Couldn't she do that at home?' [Pam looks Angela an angry look] Who else? Why don't we just warm up first? Warm up emotionally, all right?
given_circumstances: Michael believes Toby's listening method lets negativity fester, so he has gathered employees to air complaints publicly. The files contain withdrawn and redacted complaints, including sensitive material about Dwight, Angela, and Pam.
internal_thoughts: Michael reading my complaint history is bad enough, but the redacted Dwight file is dangerous. Pam notices too much, which is exactly why I do not trust her with private information. I need the room to move on before anyone connects the timing.
motivation: avoid attention on the redacted Dwight complaints and survive the public airing
reflection: Pam protected me in that moment, but then Michael read her wedding complaint and she looked at me like I was guilty. Secrets make alliances unstable.
scene_arc: Michael asks who has complained to Toby and denounces the lack of resolution. He exposes Angela's complaint pattern, mentions redacted Dwight complaints, and reads a withdrawn complaint about Pam planning her wedding on office time, which makes Pam suspect Angela.

title: Pam confronts Angela during the group grievance session | misdirected accusation
full_dialogue:
Michael: Stanley. [off camera]
Pam: [gets up and walks over to Angela, whispering to her] Hey. Thanks for ratting me out!
Michael: [still of camera] You got a lot of anger under there buddy. Come on start us out. Unleash it.
Angela: I didn't do it! [Michael and Stanley continue to talk off camera, but it's inaudible]
Pam: I find that hard to believe... considering you have problems with every single person in this entire office except Bobblehead Joe.
given_circumstances: Pam thinks Angela complained about her wedding planning and is now mixing hurt, embarrassment, and suspicion. Michael is simultaneously trying to pull Stanley into the public conflict process.
internal_thoughts: People here confuse rudeness with honesty and vulgarity with personality. I maintain standards because someone has to. If others feel judged, that may be because they are behaving in ways worth judging.
motivation: preserve standards, privacy, and moral authority in a messy office
reflection: The office is too casual with private matters and too careless with taste. I will keep my composure because standards are not decided by majority vote.
scene_arc: Pam whispers angrily to Angela and accuses her of ratting her out. Angela denies it, but Pam points to Angela's history of having problems with nearly everyone except Dwight.

title: Phyllis and Angela pretend to settle parking tension | hostile politeness
full_dialogue:
Phyllis: I know you keep saying it's your space, even though there's no assigned parking, but I keep forgetting.
Angela: Yes, that's the problem.
Phyllis: I guess so...
Michael: Okay, well... all settled, then.
Phyllis: [whispering to Angela] I don't like you.
given_circumstances: Michael is trying to resolve a parking-space complaint between Phyllis and Angela. The issue is minor on the surface, but it reflects deeper contempt masked by office civility.
internal_thoughts: People here confuse rudeness with honesty and vulgarity with personality. I maintain standards because someone has to. If others feel judged, that may be because they are behaving in ways worth judging.
motivation: preserve standards, privacy, and moral authority in a messy office
reflection: The office is too casual with private matters and too careless with taste. I will keep my composure because standards are not decided by majority vote.
scene_arc: Phyllis says she keeps forgetting Angela's claimed parking space despite there being no assigned parking. Michael declares the issue settled, and Phyllis privately tells Angela that she does not like her.

title: The group photo exposes the unresolved Pam-Jim complaint | emotional rupture
characters_involved: Angela, I.D. Photographer, Jim, Michael, Pam, Phyllis
full_dialogue:
I.D. Photographer: One, two, three... smile. [camera flashes, but no one smiles] Try to smile.
Michael: We resolved a lot today, everybody. Think happy thoughts.
I.D. Photographer: Alright, I'm just gonna take it on three...  whether you smile or not. One, two, three. [camera flashes]
Michael: Good, let's check that out. [looks at preview screen] Ew, okay, all right. One more. We'll take one more.
I.D. Photographer: That'll be another 20.
Michael: What?
Pam: Angela, I want to talk to you about something.
Michael: [off camera] You just press the button.
Angela: What?
Jim: No, Pam.
Pam: [looks at to Jim] I am. [looks at Angela] It's about the Save The Date.
Jim: Pam, it wasn't her.
Pam: What?!
Jim: I'm the one who complained about you.
Jim: I... I didn't know that Toby was gonna write it down. [the camera flashes] I was just venting.
Michael: [off camera] Okay, good. Check that out.
Jim: You know, it was one day.
Michael: [off camera] That's terrible.
Jim: And I took it right back. It was like...
Pam: Okay.
Phyllis: Oh, dear.
Michael: [off camera] Let's pay Mr. Price Gouger. [rejoins the group, on camera] Okay... we can do this. Come on, everybody. All right. Here we go. [flash goes off before he sits down]
given_circumstances: The whole office is assembled for a group photo meant to show unity. Pam still believes Angela filed the wedding complaint, and Jim knows the truth is worse for him personally.
internal_thoughts: Pam comes at me about the Save The Date, and for once I am not the guilty person in the room. That does not make me comfortable, because we both know there are other things I need hidden. Jim stepping in is a relief and an exposure at the same time.
motivation: receive Pam's confrontation without revealing the real secret underneath their tension
reflection: Being innocent of that complaint did not make me safe. Pam still knows too much about me, and now Jim has complicated her anger.
scene_arc: The photographer struggles to get smiles while Michael keeps paying for more attempts. Pam starts confronting Angela about the Save The Date, Jim stops her and admits he filed the complaint, and the photo captures the awkward fallout.
